data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== research: polyglot deterministic tooling prior-art (LSP/ast-grep/syft/scc/fail-open)
=== agent a06a4183ef7e9d0c3

All five research streams are complete with verified data. Let me synthesize into the requested deliverables.

# Prior-Art Research: Deterministic Tooling for a Plan-Review Gate

All star counts, licenses, and activity verified against the live GitHub API and primary docs on 2026-06-19.

## Up-front framing

The use cases split cleanly into two tooling worlds, and conflating them is the trap:

- **Syntactic** (parse a file, match shapes): tree-sitter, ast-grep, semgrep, ctags, scc/tokei. Cheap, in-process or single-binary, fail-open by nature, huge polyglot coverage. They locate things and count things. They **cannot** resolve imports or find true cross-file callers.
- **Semantic** (resolve a project's real symbol graph): LSP servers, SCIP indexers, stack-graphs, Glean. This is the *only* honest source for "does this import resolve?" (P2-resolution) and "who are the real callers?" (G1). All carry a daemon/index-build cost and per-language wiring.

The thing "LLM-grep gets wrong" — false "missing symbol" / "missing import" flags from text matching — is **definitionally** the semantic gap. No syntactic tool closes it; only LSP-class resolution does.

---

## (1) Comparison table

| Tool | Use cases (quality) | Languages | Integration surface | Maintenance / popularity | License | Unsupported-language behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **tree-sitter** | G2 ✅strong; P4 ✅strong; P2 ⚠️declaration-present only; G1 ❌ | One engine + N grammars; ~300+ via `tree-sitter-language-pack` | **Library** (Python/Rust/Node), in-process, no daemon | 25.9k★, MIT, v0.26.9 (2026-05), very active | MIT | No grammar → don't parse → skip. No crash surface. **Fail-open by nature** |
| **ast-grep** | G2 ✅strong; P2 ⚠️structural decls; G1 ⚠️approximation only; P4 ❌ | 33 built-in (tree-sitter), extensible | **CLI** (`sg`) + Python + Rust crate | 14.6k★, MIT, v0.43 (2026-05), active (thin bus factor) | MIT | Unmapped extension → skipped. **Fail-open** |
| **semgrep (OSS/CE)** | G2 ✅decent; P2 ⚠️; G1 ❌weak (cross-file is **paid Pro**) | ~19 GA + ~20 experimental | **CLI** (rule/YAML-oriented, heavier, slower) | 15.6k★, LGPL-2.1 (Dec-2024 relicensing → Opengrep fork), v1.167 (2026-06), company-backed | LGPL-2.1 | `generic` mode or skip. **Fail-open** |
| **universal-ctags** | P2 ✅strong **breadth**, weak precision; G1 ❌weak; G2/P4 ❌ | ~173 parsers (true C parsers + lower-quality regex optlib) | **CLI** → `tags` file. Trivial, fast | 7.2k★, GPL-2.0, 6.2.1 (2025-10) + rolling, very active | GPL-2.0 | Unknown extension → ignored. **Fail-open** |
| **LSP + multilspy** | **P2 ✅gold; G1 ✅gold**; G2 ⚠️partial; P3/P4 ❌ | 12 in multilspy / 30+ in solidlsp; quality varies per server | **Daemon orchestration** — N server processes, per-language install; multilspy unifies the API | multilspy 588★ MIT (research-grade, fragile); **solidlsp/Serena 25.5k★ MIT, very active, already in this repo** | MIT | **Must wire fail-open:** no server / crash / timeout → skip, never fail-closed |
| **SCIP + indexers** | **P2 ✅gold; G1 ✅gold** (persisted); G2/P4 ❌ | ~10 on paper; **TS + Python indexers dormant since Sept/Oct 2025**; Java/Go active | **Index-build** — per-language indexer **+ project build step** (heavier than LSP) | Protocol 652★ Apache-2.0, now `scip-code/` org, steered by Meta/Uber/Sourcegraph, active | Apache-2.0 | No indexer **or build fails** → no `.scip` → skip. Fail-open (two failure modes to handle) |
| **stack-graphs** | P2/G1 in theory; in practice ❌ | **Only 4 rule-sets** (Java/JS/Python/TS), varying quality | **Rust library**, you author TSG rules for anything else | 875★, **ARCHIVED 2025-09**, dead upstream | MIT/Apache-2.0 | No TSG → skip (but most of a repo falls into skip). **Not viable in 2026** |
| **Glean** | P2/G1 ✅powerful (+best G2-style facts) | C/C++/Hack/Haskell/Py/JS native; Go/Java/Rust/TS via SCIP ingest | **Haskell-built server + DB + indexers + Angle DSL.** Build-from-source, no releases | 1.4k★, BSD-3, very active, healthiest upstream — but **wildly over-engineered** | BSD-3 | No indexer → no facts → skip (behind a running server) |
| **syft** | **P3 ✅strong** (enumerate declared deps) | 25+ ecosystems / 40+ catalogers | **Single Go CLI**/lib, offline, integrate-once | 9.1k★, Apache-2.0, v1.45 (2026-06), very active | Apache-2.0 | No cataloger → not enumerated → succeeds. **Fail-open** |
| **osv-scanner** | P3 ❌mismatched (vuln DB ≠ existence oracle) | 16 ecosystems (lockfiles) | Single Go CLI | 10.5k★, Apache-2.0, v2.4 (2026-06), very active | Apache-2.0 | Unknown lockfile → skip. Fail-open (but wrong question for P3) |
| **dependency-cruiser** | **P2 ✅high-quality but JS/TS only** | JS/TS/CoffeeScript only — **not polyglot** | Node CLI + config + API | 6.8k★, MIT, v17.4 (2026-05), active | MIT | Non-JS → out of scope |
| **registry HTTP APIs** | P3 (the **true** existence check) | per-ecosystem (npm/PyPI/crates/Go/Maven/RubyGems/NuGet) | **N HTTP shims — the gap, opposite of integrate-once** | live services | public | Unwired ecosystem → skip |
| **scc** | **P4 ✅all three** (LOC + files + heuristic complexity + COCOMO) | ~322 | **Single Go binary**, JSON | 8.5k★, MIT, v3.7 (2026-03), very active | MIT | Unknown extension → skipped. **Fail-open** |
| **tokei** | P4 ⚠️LOC + files only, **no complexity** | 150+ | Single Rust binary, JSON | 14.6k★, MIT/Apache, v14 (2025-12), active | MIT/Apache | Skipped. Fail-open |
| **cloc** | P4 ⚠️LOC + files only, no complexity | hundreds | Perl script/CLI | 23.2k★, **GPL-2.0**, v2.08 (2026-01), active | GPL-2.0 | Ignored. Fail-open |
| **lizard** | **P4 ✅real per-function cyclomatic CCN** | ~29 | Python CLI/lib | 2.4k★, MIT, 1.23 (2026-06), active | MIT | Unsupported → not analyzed → skip. Fail-open |

---

## (2) Recommended minimal stack

Five tools cover P2/P3/G1/G2/P4 with the least integration effort and maximum polyglot reach. Three are single binaries; one is a Python lib already running in this checkout; one is a thin per-ecosystem HTTP layer you accept as irreducible.

| Use case | Primary tool | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **P2 (resolution) + G1 (real callers)** | **LSP via solidlsp** (Serena's lib, already in this repo) — multilspy as the lighter alternative | The only honest source for import/alias/re-export resolution and true callers. solidlsp is synchronous, 30+ langs, battle-tested at 25.5k★, already configured here. This is the one component that fixes the LLM-grep false-"missing" problem |
| **P2 (cheap breadth backstop) + G2** | **ast-grep** (CLI/Python, tree-sitter) | When no LSP server exists, ast-grep gives structural "is this symbol declared / where does this shape live" across 33 langs. Best G2 locus tool. tree-sitter directly if you need lower-level metrics/locus |
| **P3 (enumerate declared deps)** | **syft** (single Go CLI) | Integrate-once, 25+ ecosystems → normalized `{name, version, ecosystem}` tuples |
| **P3 (existence confirmation)** | **thin per-ecosystem registry-lookup layer** (npm/PyPI/crates/Go/Maven) fed by syft's tuples | No polyglot tool exists; wire the top ~5 ecosystems, skip the rest |
| **P4 (LOC + files + rough complexity)** | **scc** (single Go binary) | All three metrics, ~322 langs, one call. Add **lizard** only if you need real per-function CCN |

**How to wire fail-open into all of it** (this is the CRITICAL requirement):

1. **Capability map, not assumptions.** Maintain an explicit extension/ecosystem → tool map. Any path not in the map (e.g. a `.tf` Terraform file with no resolver, a Nix/Bazel/COBOL repo) → the check returns **`skipped` / "not assessable"**, which the gate treats as a *pass*, never a violation.
2. **LSP/SCIP failure modes downgrade to skip.** No server registered → skip. Server crash / spawn failure / missing toolchain → skip. Bounded per-request **timeout** → skip on expiry. (multilspy's own release notes are mostly about servers hanging / JDTLS aborting — these *will* happen; catch and degrade.) Never block the gate on a slow or absent server.
3. **Confidence labelling, not binary verdicts.** A "definition not found" from a *weak* server (jedi, Solargraph, terraform-ls) is **low-confidence**, not proof of a defect. Only emit a hard "missing" flag from a top-tier server (pyright/gopls/rust-analyzer/typescript-language-server/clangd). Otherwise downgrade to "could not confirm."
4. **Syntactic tools are inherently fail-open** — unknown extension → not parsed → no output → no false finding. scc/tokei/ast-grep/ctags/syft all do this natively; no extra wiring needed beyond not treating "empty result" as "violation."
5. **The gate's contract:** a check produces `{pass | fail | skipped}`. `skipped` and infra-failure both resolve to non-blocking. A `fail` is only ever emitted with a positive, high-confidence signal — never from absence of evidence.

---

## (3) Honest gaps — where no good low-effort polyglot tool exists (LLM-with-tool-access fallback)

1. **P3 registry existence is irreducibly N-way.** syft enumerates declared deps polyglot and integrate-once, but **no single tool confirms a *new* dep exists in its registry.** That's per-ecosystem HTTP (different URLs, shapes, rate limits — crates.io needs a User-Agent and throttles to ~1 req/s). osv-scanner is a semantic mismatch: OSV is a *vulnerability* DB, so absence means "no known CVE" (the common, healthy case), **not** "doesn't exist." Wire the top ecosystems; **fall back to an LLM with web/registry tool access** for the long tail.

2. **G1 (real callers) collapses to ground-truth only where a strong LSP server or live SCIP indexer exists.** ast-grep/semgrep-OSS/ctags give *syntactic approximations* — candidate call-site lists that can't distinguish two same-named symbols or follow aliases. Semgrep's real cross-file analysis is **paid Pro**. For languages with no good server (much of the long tail, plus weak servers like terraform-ls), there is **no deterministic G1**. This is the strongest case for an **LLM-with-tool-access fallback**: hand it ast-grep/grep candidates + file reads and let it reason about which are real callers, clearly labelled lower-confidence than an LSP `textDocument/references` result.

3. **Polyglot "import resolves to a real installed package/file" has no unified tool.** dependency-cruiser is excellent but **JS/TS only**. Import resolution is per-language by nature. LSP go-to-definition is the closest polyglot mechanism but needs a configured server per language and answers project-local resolution, not registry existence.

4. **stack-graphs is dead (archived Sept 2025); Glean is over-engineered; SCIP's headline TS/Python indexers are dormant.** Do not build on stack-graphs. Glean only pays off at Meta scale. SCIP works for Java/Go but its frozen TS/Python indexers mean LSP is the better live-resolution choice for those two — which is convenient, since solidlsp is already running in this repo.

5. **scc's "complexity" is a keyword heuristic, not McCabe CCN, and per-file not per-function.** Fine as a relative hotspot signal; do not report it as true cyclomatic complexity. Add lizard (~29 langs) for real per-function CCN where it matters; accept the heuristic elsewhere.

**Net recommendation:** lean on the LSP layer you already have (solidlsp/Serena) for the semantic core (P2/G1), add three single-binary syntactic tools (ast-grep for G2, syft for P3-enumerate, scc for P4), accept a thin per-ecosystem HTTP shim for P3-existence, and route everything else — weak/missing-server G1, long-tail registry existence — to an explicitly lower-confidence LLM-with-tools fallback. Wire every check to emit `skipped` (= pass) on any unsupported language or infra failure, and only ever emit a hard `fail` from a high-confidence positive signal.
